questions.q: Who founded the Maratha power in the 17th century?
questions.explanation: The Maratha power was founded by Shivaji in the 17th century CE.

questions.q: In which century did the Marathas grow into a vast confederacy dominating much of India?
questions.explanation: The Marathas dominated much of India as a confederacy during the 18th century CE.

questions.q: The Maratha state is associated with the ideal of 'Hindavi Swarajya', meaning:
questions.explanation: Hindavi Swarajya, the ideal of self-rule, was a defining concept of the Maratha state.

questions.q: In the 18th century, real power in the Maratha Empire passed to hereditary prime ministers called the:
questions.explanation: The Peshwas, the hereditary prime ministers, became the effective leaders of the Maratha Empire.

questions.q: The Marathas were a principal rival of the Mughals and which European power?
questions.explanation: The Marathas were major rivals of both the Mughals and the British in India.

questions.q: The Maratha Empire was based principally in which region of India?
questions.explanation: The Maratha Empire originated in western India, centred on the Maharashtra region.

questions.q: What hereditary title did the rulers descended from Shivaji hold at the head of the Maratha state?
questions.explanation: The Maratha sovereigns, beginning with Shivaji, bore the title Chhatrapati.

questions.q: The Marathas were famed for which fast-moving branch of their military?
questions.explanation: The Marathas were renowned for their swift light cavalry, ideal for rapid raids.

questions.q: Which heavy 1761 defeat checked Maratha expansion in the north?
questions.explanation: The Marathas suffered a great defeat at the Third Battle of Panipat in 1761 against Ahmad Shah Durrani.

questions.q: By the early 19th century the Maratha confederacy was finally subdued by which power?
questions.explanation: The Maratha confederacy was defeated and absorbed by the British East India Company in the Anglo-Maratha Wars.

questions.q: Which Peshwa, prime minister from 1720 to 1740, expanded Maratha power across northern India and is said never to have lost a battle?
questions.explanation: Baji Rao I led rapid cavalry campaigns north and is remembered as an undefeated general.

questions.q: Who was the first Peshwa to make the office hereditary, founding the Bhat family's dominance?
questions.explanation: Balaji Vishwanath established the hereditary line of Peshwas that led the empire in the 18th century.

questions.q: Which city became the de facto capital of the Maratha Empire under the Peshwas?
questions.explanation: Pune served as the principal seat of the Peshwas and the heart of Maratha power in the 18th century.

questions.q: Which two financial levies did the Marathas commonly impose on territories they raided or controlled?
questions.explanation: The Marathas collected chauth (one-fourth of revenue) and sardeshmukhi (an additional levy) from their domains.

questions.q: The Holkar dynasty of the Maratha confederacy ruled from which central Indian city?
questions.explanation: The Holkars governed the Malwa region from Indore as one of the great Maratha houses.

questions.q: The Scindia (Shinde) house of the Maratha confederacy was centred on which city?
questions.explanation: The Scindias of Gwalior were among the most powerful chiefs of the Maratha confederacy.

questions.q: The Gaekwad dynasty of the Maratha confederacy ruled from which western city?
questions.explanation: The Gaekwads ruled the state of Baroda (Vadodara) as a leading Maratha house.

questions.q: The Bhonsle branch of the confederacy, distinct from Shivaji's line, ruled which city in central India?
questions.explanation: The Bhonsles of Nagpur formed one of the five major chiefdoms of the Maratha confederacy.

questions.q: Which young Peshwa is credited with reviving Maratha fortunes after the disaster at Panipat in 1761?
questions.explanation: Peshwa Madhavrao I restored Maratha strength in the years after the defeat at Panipat.

questions.q: Which Maratha statesman rebuilt the empire's power in the north after Panipat and dominated the Mughal court at Delhi?
questions.explanation: Mahadji Scindia restored Maratha influence in northern India and became the power behind the Mughal throne.

questions.q: The series of three wars between the Marathas and the British is collectively known as the:
questions.explanation: The Marathas and British fought three Anglo-Maratha Wars between 1775 and 1818.

questions.q: Which Holkar queen of Malwa, ruling from Maheshwar, became renowned for just governance and temple-building?
questions.explanation: Ahilyabai Holkar ruled Malwa from Maheshwar and was celebrated for piety and public works.

questions.q: Which queen led Maratha resistance against Aurangzeb after the death of Rajaram in the long War of 27 Years?
questions.explanation: Tarabai rallied the Marathas against Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns following Rajaram's death in 1700.

questions.q: Which 1802 treaty brought the Peshwa under British protection, triggering the Second Anglo-Maratha War?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Bassein in 1802 made Peshwa Baji Rao II a subsidiary ally of the British, prompting Maratha resistance.

questions.q: The First Anglo-Maratha War ended with which 1782 treaty?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Salbai in 1782 concluded the First Anglo-Maratha War, restoring an uneasy peace.

questions.q: Which influential minister at Pune effectively guided Maratha affairs during the minority of the young Peshwa in the late 18th century?
questions.explanation: Nana Phadnavis, a leading statesman, steered Maratha policy from Pune in the late 1700s.

questions.q: Who was the last Peshwa, surrendering to the British in 1818 at the close of the Third Anglo-Maratha War?
questions.explanation: Baji Rao II, the last Peshwa, surrendered in 1818 and was pensioned off to Bithur, ending Peshwa rule.

questions.q: In which 1737 raid did Baji Rao I strike at the very outskirts of the Mughal capital?
questions.explanation: Baji Rao I led a swift cavalry raid to the outskirts of Delhi in 1737, alarming the Mughal court.

questions.q: Which Maratha admiral defended the Konkan coast and battled European fleets in the early 18th century?
questions.explanation: Kanhoji Angre commanded the Maratha navy and resisted British, Dutch, and Portuguese ships along the Konkan coast.

questions.q: Which Portuguese-held fort north of Mumbai did the Marathas under Chimaji Appa capture in 1739?
questions.explanation: Chimaji Appa, brother of Baji Rao I, took the Portuguese fort of Bassein (Vasai) in 1739 after a hard siege.
